does: This paper proposes SAE-FD, a continual learning method that uses sparse autoencoders to improve how large language models learn new tasks without forgetting old ones. The core innovation is anchoring model representations in the sparse feature space discovered by a pre-trained sparse autoencoder, where dense neural activations are decomposed into a sparse overcomplete basis of interpretable features. By operating regularization in this sparser space rather than dense weight or gradient spaces, the method enables more targeted protection of previously learned knowledge while allowing flexible learning of new task-relevant features. The authors evaluate SAE-FD on two continual learning benchmarks across three model architectures, demonstrating consistent improvements in balancing performance on new tasks against retention of old-task knowledge.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental challenge in continual learning, building on decades of research into catastrophic forgetting and regularization-based solutions that constrain model updates in various representation spaces. It extends prior continual learning approaches including elastic weight consolidation and other regularization methods that operate in weight, gradient, or output spaces, which the paper identifies as suffering from feature superposition. The research also connects to emerging work on sparse autoencoders as interpretability tools for understanding large language models, repurposing them here as a structural innovation for disentangling learned representations. The paper directly responds to known limitations of dense representation spaces in continual learning, leveraging recent insights about feature superposition and overcomplete basis representations to achieve more granular control over knowledge preservation.
enables: This work opens pathways toward more efficient and selective continual learning in large language models by demonstrating that operating in sparser, more interpretable feature spaces can decouple learning of new information from protection of old knowledge. Future research could explore whether sparse autoencoder-based regularization generalizes across diverse model scales and architectures, investigate end-to-end training of sparse features alongside task learning, or apply similar disentanglement principles to other continual learning scenarios beyond language models. The approach may also enable better inspection and understanding of what knowledge is preserved versus forgotten during continual adaptation, supporting both safer model deployment and more interpretable learning dynamics in foundational models.
